proposals.title: Hallucinated Index File Name Not Present in Query Guide
proposals.detect: Generated code opens a binary index file (e.g., lineitem_orderkey_positions.bin) that is NOT listed in the Query Guide's Index Layouts section. stderr shows 'No such file or directory' at runtime. Validation is skipped for that iteration.
proposals.fix: Before emitting any fopen/mmap call for a binary index file, verify the exact filename appears verbatim in the Query Guide's Index Layouts section. Never infer or construct index file names from column names, join patterns, or naming conventions. If no matching index exists in the Query Guide, build the join structure at runtime instead of attempting to open an index file.
proposals.evidence: Q18 iter_1: stderr='lineitem_orderkey_positions.bin: No such file or directory'. This index was never built. One optimization cycle wasted.

proposals.title: Variable Scope Error: Conditional-Block Variables Referenced in Unconditional Cleanup
proposals.detect: Compile error: 'X was not declared in this scope' where X is a file descriptor, mmap pointer, or size variable. Root cause: the variable was declared inside an if-block (e.g., 'if (use_index) { int fd = open(...); void* ptr = mmap(...); }') but referenced in cleanup code (munmap/close) outside that block. Pattern appears when the optimizer refactors code to conditionally use an index while cleanup remains unconditional.
proposals.fix: Declare all file descriptor and mmap pointer variables (fd_*, sz_*, ptr_*) at function scope BEFORE any conditional blocks, initialized to -1/0/nullptr. The conditional block only assigns them. Cleanup code then checks validity: 'if (fd >= 0) close(fd); if (ptr != nullptr) munmap(ptr, sz);'. Never declare resource variables inside a conditional branch if they are closed/freed outside it.
proposals.evidence: Q18 iter_5 compile errors: 'ord_raw was not declared in this scope' (line 310), 'sz_ord_idx was not declared in this scope' (line 402), 'fd_ord_idx was not declared in this scope' (line 402). Compile failure, validation skipped.

proposals.title: Missing GENDB_PHASE Instrumentation for Aggregation and Sort Phases
proposals.detect: Query total time >> sum of all GENDB_PHASE timings (gap >10% of total). Specifically: query has GROUP BY or ORDER BY, but no GENDB_PHASE('aggregation') or GENDB_PHASE('sort_topk') wraps the grouping loop or sort call. The optimizer receives no diagnostic for the dominant bottleneck and targets other, smaller phases instead.
proposals.fix: Every query with GROUP BY must wrap the aggregation loop in GENDB_PHASE('aggregation'). Every query with ORDER BY or LIMIT must wrap the sort/partial_sort/heap call in GENDB_PHASE('sort_topk'). These must be present from iter_0. Check: sum of all GENDB_PHASE timings should be within 5% of [TIMING] total. If the gap exceeds 10%, there is an uninstrumented phase.
proposals.evidence: Q9 iter_0: phases sum=658 ms, total=7337 ms. Gap=6679 ms (91% of runtime) from uninstrumented GROUP BY nation/year aggregation over 60M LINEITEM rows. Optimizer targeted build_joins (565 ms visible) instead of the real bottleneck. Took 4 iterations to discover the true bottleneck.

proposals.title: Strengthen P12: Immediate Revert on Regression, Explicit Direction Change
proposals.detect: Any iteration that regresses >20% from the current best timing. Current P12 says 'track best, revert if regression'. Observed behavior shows the revert is not triggering quickly enough: Q6 accumulated 4 consecutive regressions (iters 2-5) without reverting to iter_1 best; Q3 hit an 8.5x regression at iter_5 (414ms -> 3512ms) caused by aggregation_merge exploding.
proposals.fix: Strengthen the revert trigger: (1) Revert threshold should be >15% regression (not >20%). (2) When reverting, the optimizer MUST explicitly log which phase regressed (compare per-phase timings, not just total) and annotate the next iteration with 'do not modify [phase_name]'. (3) If the same phase causes regression in 2 consecutive iters, freeze that phase entirely for the remaining iterations. (4) For P3/P1 aggregation rewrites: if aggregation_merge increases by more than 2x, immediately suspect a std::map or contended parallel-map regression.
proposals.evidence: Q6: best 101 ms at iter_1; iters 2-5 were 129/147/171/152 ms — 4 consecutive regressions, no revert to 101 ms. Q3: aggregation_merge exploded 31x at iter_5 (33ms -> 1038ms); delivered best was still 414ms but one cycle wasted.

proposals.title: Index Strategy Instability in Multi-Join Queries
proposals.detect: index_load phase timing varies by >3x across iterations (e.g., 654ms -> 1418ms -> 2404ms -> 0ms). The optimizer alternates between using pre-built binary indexes and building runtime hash maps for the same join key, causing unpredictable regressions. When index_load drops to 0ms, a sibling phase (build_joins) correspondingly spikes.
proposals.fix: Once a query iteration successfully uses a pre-built index (index_load > 0, timing improved), that index file path must be preserved in all subsequent iterations. The optimizer should not re-evaluate whether to use the index vs. build at runtime — the decision should be locked in after the first successful use. Specifically: track per-file index decisions as sticky state. If build_joins spikes >2x when index_load drops to 0, the optimizer regressed by dropping the index.
proposals.evidence: Q9: index_load trajectory: iter_2=1418ms, iter_3=2404ms (regression), iter_4=654ms (best), iter_5=0ms (dropped) -> build_joins jumped from 206ms to 1806ms. Total regressed from 1197ms to 2120ms.

Q6.notes: 4 consecutive regressions after iter_1, never recovered

Q9.notes: 91% runtime uninstrumented in iter_0; index strategy instability

Q18.notes: iter_1 runtime fail (hallucinated index), iter_5 compile fail (variable scope)
